speaker: Speaker 1
text: Hello and welcome to Yoga by Nature podcast.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: The following is a whole restorative class, usually I just give you a little bite of one, and but this is the entire class for you to enjoy.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: And um, it's a recording of a live class that I did on Zoom, and this was actually recorded back in October.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: And it was designed as a class to help to balance the the sort of high um or wind energy that we get in October and the autumn in general.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: When um the uh the creative energy and the sort of ideas machine tends to be very high, there's there's often a lot of movement in nature, lots of wind.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: Um and uh it's generally a time of year that people can find a bit unsettling.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: As is Christmas.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: And butter energy is uh is is one of the Ayurvedic doshas, and it forms one of the three, and the other ones are and water.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: um

speaker: Speaker 1
text: Pitter, which is the fire dosha, the heat dosha, and the other one is kuffer, which is Earth and and water.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: And um, yeah, they uh, they're related.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: They're related to the elements.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: Um, so butter energy, it's not bad, uh we all have a little bit of butter and we all need a little bit of butter, but it's the first one that tends to go out of balance.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: And um, I see that during December when people are so preoccupied with all their preparations, all the and all their social things and all their shopping and

speaker: Speaker 1
text: um

speaker: Speaker 1
text: It's such a it's such a wonderful exciting time of year, people preparing to spend time together or just treat themselves

speaker: Speaker 1
text: or enjoy the journey into winter for those of you who enjoy it.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: I know not everybody does.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: And this is an offering which can help to just form a a bit of a balance.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: A counterpoint.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: to that high um butter energy.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: And it's a very grounding breathy practice.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: So we only do about four postures in the whole hour.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: They're classic restorative postures and so we set up to be really comfortable in the posture and then I talk you through the breath.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: Um and it's not a strong pranayama practice at all.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: That's not my style at all with pranayama.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: Uh so we're not doing, you know, punishing ratios or anything like that.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: We're doing uh an exploration of the breath.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: And the breath is so powerful and um this ability that we have

speaker: Speaker 1
text: to sort of guide and alter the breath has a huge really profound effect on the the nervous system.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: So when we're in this state of sympathetic arousal, fight or flight, that we're often in, especially this time of year,

speaker: Speaker 1
text: um, just helping to switch on that parasympathetic nervous system by way of the breath, can just give you that few moments to replenish that you need.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: To go well, at whatever pace you go, uh for the rest of December, maybe it's health for leather.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: Or maybe you have a more relaxed approach, but it should go.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: Some way to giving you the energy you need to get through to January.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: So, as always, this goes out from me to you with lots of love.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: And I really hope you enjoy it and benefit from it.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: And um, yeah, just let me say about props.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: Your usual props, you'll need a bolster, a couple of blocks, a blanket, maybe a belt if you like to bind uh for reclining cobbler's pose.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: I will be instructing as if you can see me for a little bit, so you have to forgive me for that.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: Um and if you are not sure, then just you can just look up online.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: You can Google the pose that I'm talking about and you should be able to get a visual image.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: Um if you're not so familiar with the practice.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: Okay.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: So any questions or comments, do get in touch with me.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: I'm Maureen and my email is info@yogabynature.org.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: Or you can find my website, which is www.yogabynature.org.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: And um

speaker: Speaker 1
text: And it's always lovely to hear from you.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: Uh but if I don't, I still know that you're out there doing this and that this podcast is getting to the places that it needs to get to, so thank you.

speaker: Speaker 2

text: sometimes when you change the breath, your nervous system can start to react, um if you, if you push it too far.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: Um so just know that if it feels agitating at all in any way what we're doing then.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: It just means you're pushing a bit too hard, so just back off until you feel comfortable again.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: It's supposed to feel easy.

text: All right, so let's begin in Badana, our reclining cobbler's pose.

speaker: Speaker 2

speaker: Speaker 2

speaker: Speaker 2
text: So you're sort of leaning back, use the support of your hands, so you can very gently guide yourself back, you're arching back over the bolster.

text: So this session is all about the breath.

text: This is a really nice pose for discovering the breath.

text: We're going to do a journey through the body.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: Starting at the pelvic floor.
